1996.assessment:
Egypt carried a heavy debt burden in the mid-1990s at approximately 87% of GDP.
Chronic budget deficits of 7-9% of GDP driven by energy subsidies, public sector
wages, and debt service. The IMF-backed economic reform program (ERSAP) initiated
in 1991 had reduced debt from over 100% in the late 1980s but fiscal consolidation
remained incomplete. Egypt benefited from debt relief following Gulf War participation.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

1997.assessment:
Gradual debt reduction continued. Budget deficit remained elevated at ~7% of GDP.
Luxor tourist massacre in November 1997 caused temporary fiscal stress through
reduced tourism revenues. Energy subsidy bill remained a major expenditure.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

1998.assessment:
Continued gradual fiscal improvement. Oil revenues supported government finances.
Privatization proceeds temporarily reduced deficit. External debt service remained
a significant fiscal burden.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

1999.assessment:
Debt-to-GDP continued declining as nominal GDP grew steadily. Fiscal deficit
averaged 3-4% of GDP during this period due to partial subsidy reforms and
privatization revenue. Egypt's fiscal position was among its better periods
of the Mubarak era.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2000.assessment:
Debt-to-GDP fell below 80% briefly. Budget deficit around 4-5% of GDP. Tourism
boom and natural gas exports supported fiscal position. However, structural
spending pressures from subsidies and public sector wages persisted.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2001.assessment:
Debt-to-GDP edged up following global slowdown post-dot-com bust. Egypt's GDP
growth slowed. 9/11 impacts on tourism revenue created fiscal pressures. Deficit
widened to approximately 6% of GDP.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2002.assessment:
Debt ratio rose as Egyptian pound came under pressure ahead of the 2003 float.
Fiscal deficit widened as revenues fell in dollar terms and subsidy costs rose.
The period 2001-2003 was one of fiscal deterioration driven by currency pressures.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2003.assessment:
Debt-to-GDP surged above 90% following the January 2003 Egyptian pound float,
which caused a sharp nominal devaluation (~35%). External debt denominated in
foreign currency increased in local currency terms. Budget deficit reached ~9%
of GDP. This was a peak year for fiscal stress in the early 2000s.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database
World Bank Egypt Country Economic Memorandum 2005

2004.assessment:
Debt ratio near peak following devaluation. Ahmed Nazif government (appointed July
2004) launched economic reform agenda including tax reform (flat 20% income tax),
customs reductions, and privatization acceleration. These reforms would gradually
improve fiscal position from 2005-2008.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2005.assessment:
Debt-to-GDP began declining as nominal GDP growth accelerated on the back of
commodity prices and economic reforms. Tax reform implementation boosted revenues.
Budget deficit fell to approximately 8% of GDP as growth outpaced debt accumulation.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2006.assessment:
Continued fiscal improvement driven by strong GDP growth (7%+) and Suez Canal
revenues. Natural gas export revenues supplemented budget. However, energy subsidies
consumed approximately 5-6% of GDP, limiting consolidation.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2007.assessment:
Debt ratio fell sharply as GDP growth reached 7.1%. Strong Suez Canal revenues,
natural gas exports, and FDI boom improved the fiscal position. Budget deficit
narrowed to approximately 7% of GDP. Energy subsidy bill spiked due to rising
global oil prices but was offset by growing revenues.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2008.assessment:
Debt-to-GDP reached its lowest level since the early 1990s at approximately 73%,
driven by robust 7.2% GDP growth and high commodity export revenues. However,
global food price crisis (bread riots, April 2008) forced emergency spending on
food subsidies. Budget deficit widened to ~8% of GDP due to subsidy costs despite
improved revenues.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2009.assessment:
Debt ratio rose as global financial crisis reduced Suez Canal revenues, FDI, and
tourism. GDP growth slowed to 4.7%. Budget deficit widened to ~8.5% of GDP as
revenue fell while spending continued. This began a fiscal deterioration trend
that would continue through the political transition.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2010.assessment:
Debt ratio continued rising. GDP growth recovered to 5.1% but budget deficit
remained elevated at ~8% of GDP. Energy and food subsidies consumed over 8% of GDP.
Fiscal position was deteriorating heading into the January 2011 revolution.
Egypt's public finances were structurally weak with chronic deficits.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2011.assessment:
Revolution year caused sharp fiscal deterioration. Tourism collapsed (revenues fell
by over $1 billion), FDI dried up, and GDP growth fell to 1.8%. Government
spending on security and emergency measures increased. Budget deficit widened to
~10% of GDP. Egypt lost $6 billion in foreign reserves in 3 months. SCAF
transition created fiscal uncertainty and investor flight.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database
World Bank Egypt Economic Monitor 2012

2012.assessment:
Continued fiscal deterioration under the transitional period and Morsi presidency.
GDP growth remained subdued at 2.2%. Budget deficit reached ~11% of GDP. Morsi
government attempted IMF loan negotiations (cancelled November 2012). Energy
subsidy reform was politically impossible. Foreign reserves fell to dangerously
low levels of ~$13 billion (under 3 months import cover).

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2013.assessment:
Debt crossed 90% threshold. Coup year created additional fiscal uncertainty but
was immediately followed by $12 billion in Gulf aid (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait)
that stabilized foreign reserves and allowed budget financing. Budget deficit
reached ~13% of GDP — highest since the early 1990s. GDP growth fell to 2.1%.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2014.assessment:
Debt remained elevated above 90%. Continued Gulf financial support maintained
fiscal stability. Sisi government began energy subsidy reform: fuel subsidies
reduced by 40% in July 2014 (first major reform in decades). Budget deficit
reduced to ~10% of GDP. Growth recovered to 2.9%.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2015.assessment:
Debt began slow decline from peak. Subsidy reform continued. Egypt held Sharm
el-Sheikh investment conference (March 2015), securing $36 billion in pledges.
Budget deficit fell to ~9.5% of GDP. Growth reached 4.4%. However, October 2015
Metrojet bombing over Sinai crashed tourism revenues, creating new fiscal pressure.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2016.assessment:
Debt spiked above 97% of GDP as foreign reserve crisis deepened. Black market
dollar premium reached 100%. Ahead of November 2016 pound float, Egypt secured
$12 billion IMF Extended Fund Facility (November 2016). Gulf aid slowed as donors
awaited IMF deal. The pre-float period was a severe fiscal and balance-of-payments
crisis. Egypt introduced VAT (September 2016).

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database
IMF Press Release No. 16/487, $12 billion EFF for Egypt

2017.assessment:
Debt-to-GDP peaked above 100% as the November 2016 devaluation (pound lost ~50%
of value) increased external debt in local currency terms. Budget deficit reached
~11% of GDP. IMF program required fiscal tightening including further subsidy
cuts, VAT implementation, and civil service reforms. Despite near-term pain,
inflation reached 23.5% and real wages collapsed, undermining social stability.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2018.assessment:
Debt began declining from peak as IMF program achieved results. GDP growth
accelerated to 5.3%. Further subsidy cuts: electricity subsidies phased out,
fuel subsidy cuts. Budget deficit fell to ~9.5% of GDP. Primary fiscal surplus
achieved for first time in years. Tourism recovery (Sharm reopened to some markets).

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2019.assessment:
Continued debt reduction. IMF EFF program completed. GDP growth reached 5.6%,
highest since 2008. Budget deficit fell to ~8% of GDP. Egypt secured a $2.7 billion
IMF Stand-By Arrangement as precautionary successor to the EFF. Suez Canal
revenues recovered, FDI improved. Egypt was a rare success story in MENA for
fiscal consolidation.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 caused tourism collapse (revenues fell ~70%) and remittance decline.
However, Egypt avoided deep recession (GDP growth: 3.6%, one of few countries
with positive growth). Emergency IMF Rapid Financing Instrument ($2.77 billion,
May 2020) provided quick liquidity. Budget deficit widened to ~8.5% of GDP but
debt ratio remained roughly stable due to pound stability relative to 2016.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2021.assessment:
Debt ratio declined as post-COVID recovery took hold. GDP growth reached 3.3%.
Tourism began recovering. Budget deficit fell to ~7.8% of GDP. Egypt's fiscal
management was assessed positively by IMF and rating agencies. New Alamein City
and other mega-projects created off-budget fiscal risks.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2022.assessment:
Russia-Ukraine war caused multiple shocks: grain price spike (Egypt world's largest
wheat importer), Russian and Ukrainian tourist loss, and global financial tightening
triggered $20 billion in portfolio outflows from Egypt's treasury bills. Egypt was
forced to devalue the pound three times (March, October, November 2022). IMF
emergency discussions began. Debt ratio edged up as external obligations increased.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2023.assessment:
Debt rose above 92% as multiple devaluations raised external debt in local currency
terms. Egypt agreed $3 billion IMF EFF in December 2022 (later increased to $8 billion
in March 2024). Inflation peaked at 33.9% creating severe social stress. Budget deficit
approximately 7.5% of GDP. Suez Canal revenues reduced by Red Sea shipping diversions
due to Houthi attacks from late 2023.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database

2024.assessment:
IMF program expanded to $8 billion in March 2024, alongside UAE $35 billion Ras
el-Hekma investment deal that provided immediate foreign exchange relief. Pound
devalued further in March 2024, losing ~60% of its value. Interest rate raised to
27.25%. Debt ratio projected to decline slightly as GDP growth and external inflows
stabilize. Budget deficit target approximately 7% of GDP under IMF conditionality.
Suez Canal revenues significantly reduced by Red Sea crisis (40% drop in traffic).

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, April 2024

2025.assessment:
IMF projection for 2025 shows modest debt reduction if program remains on track.
Growth projected at approximately 4-5%. Key risks include Red Sea shipping
disruption (Houthi attacks reduce Suez revenues), ongoing Gaza conflict spillovers,
and need to maintain subsidy reform trajectory. Egypt's fiscal consolidation
remains fragile and dependent on Gulf support and IMF program compliance.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, April 2024

1996.assessment:
Egypt's GDP per capita was approximately $1,261 in 1996 (current USD).
The economy was stabilizing after the early 1990s reforms under the
IMF/World Bank structural adjustment program. Growth was modest but
positive. Tourism and Suez Canal revenues were the main foreign
exchange earners. The economy was growing steadily during Mubarak's
second and third term.

1997.assessment:
GDP per capita continued rising to $1,341 in 1997. The Egyptian
economy was on a positive growth trajectory. Tourism declined
sharply after the Luxor massacre (November 1997) but overall GDP
was still positive for the year as other sectors compensated.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

1998.assessment:
GDP per capita reached $1,368 in 1998. Growth remained positive.
Tourism began recovering. Remittances from Egyptians working abroad
were a steady source of income. The economy was growing moderately.

GDP per capita rose to $1,458 in 1999. The Egyptian economy was growing
steadily. Tourism recovered from the Luxor setback. Gas exports were
beginning to grow as Egypt's natural gas sector developed.

recovery. However, the Egyptian pound came under currency pressure

GDP per capita fell sharply to $1,038 in 2003 due to the Egyptian
pound devaluation (January 2003 float: pound lost ~50% vs. USD).
In real terms, the economy grew, but in USD terms the collapse in
the exchange rate dramatically reduced per capita income. Tourism
was further disrupted by the Iraq War.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2004.assessment:
GDP per capita recovered to $1,137 in 2004 as the pound stabilized
and the economy began strong recovery. Egypt entered a period of
rapid economic growth (6-7% annually) under the Nazif government's
reform program. Privatization accelerated. Tourism recovered.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2005.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to $1,268 in 2005. The Egyptian economy was in
a boom phase with 6-7% real growth. Oil and gas exports increased.
Tourism recovered fully. FDI inflows surged under privatization.
Egypt was experiencing its strongest economic performance in years.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2006.assessment:
GDP per capita grew to $1,476 in 2006. Strong economic growth continued
at 6.8%. The period 2004-2008 was Egypt's best economic performance
in decades, driven by high oil prices, FDI inflows, tourism, and
Suez Canal revenues. However, growth benefited elites disproportionately.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2007.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to $1,791 in 2007. Egypt experienced its highest
real GDP growth rate in decades (~7.1%). High oil prices, FDI boom,
tourism at peak, Suez Canal revenues all contributed. The global
commodity boom also helped Egyptian exporters. The economy was near
its pre-revolution peak.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2008.assessment:
GDP per capita reached $2,160 in 2008, a multi-year high. Egypt
was at the peak of its pre-revolution economic performance. Real
GDP growth was ~7.2%. High global oil prices boosted revenues.
However, food price inflation spiked, causing bread riots in
April 2008. Growth was not sufficiently inclusive.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2009.assessment:
GDP per capita rose further to $2,462 in 2009. Despite the global
financial crisis, Egypt's economy grew at ~4.7%. The country was
somewhat insulated from the global crisis by its limited financial
integration. Tourism suffered from global recession. The economy
was approaching its pre-revolution peak in per capita terms.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2010.assessment:
GDP per capita reached $2,803 in 2010, the pre-revolution peak.
Real growth was ~5.1%. The economy was performing well in aggregate
terms but distributional problems, unemployment (especially youth),
corruption, and inequality fueled revolutionary discontent. The
gap between macroeconomic performance and lived experience was extreme.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2011.assessment:
GDP per capita was approximately $2,847 in 2011 in current USD terms
(slightly above 2010 due to exchange rate). However, real GDP growth
slumped to 1.8% as the revolution disrupted tourism, investment, and
production. Tourism collapsed. FDI fell sharply. The nominal USD figure
masks the severe real economic disruption.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2012.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to $3,217 in 2012 in nominal terms. Real growth
was 2.2%. The Morsi government struggled to manage the economy amid
political instability. IMF negotiations for a loan failed. Foreign
reserves fell sharply. Tourism remained well below pre-revolution levels.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2013.assessment:
GDP per capita was $3,347 in 2013. Real growth was only 2.1% amid
the political crisis. The military coup (July 2013) was followed by
emergency Gulf state aid ($12 billion from Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait)
which stabilized the economy. Tourism continued to decline from
political instability and security concerns.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2014.assessment:
GDP per capita reached $3,476 in 2014. Growth was 2.9%, supported
by Gulf state aid and gradual stabilization under Sisi. Energy
subsidies were cut, freeing budget resources. Tourism remained
depressed. The economy was recovering but below its growth potential.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2015.assessment:
GDP per capita reached its peak of $3,547 in 2015. Real growth
was 4.4%. Gulf aid continued. The Suez Canal expansion was completed.
However, tourism collapsed after the Russian Metrojet bombing (October
2015) and ISIS-Sinai attack on the plane. This would soon be reflected
in subsequent year declines.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2016.assessment:
GDP per capita fell to $3,478 in 2016. The IMF agreed to a $12 billion
loan (November 2016). Egypt floated the pound in November 2016, causing
immediate ~50% devaluation against the USD. Real growth was 4.3% but
the devaluation reduced USD-denominated GDP per capita. Inflation spiked.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2017.assessment:
GDP per capita fell sharply to $2,412 in 2017, largely reflecting
the full-year effect of the November 2016 pound devaluation. The
pound lost approximately 50% of its value against the dollar.
Real GDP growth was 4.2% — a positive signal — but living standards
in USD terms deteriorated dramatically. Inflation peaked at ~23.5%.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2018.assessment:
GDP per capita recovered slightly to $2,548 in 2018 as the pound
stabilized somewhat and real growth reached 5.3%. The IMF program
continued. Tourism began recovering. However, living standards were
still well below 2015 peak. Inflation remained elevated at ~14%.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2019.assessment:
GDP per capita recovered to $2,982 in 2019 with real growth of 5.6%.
Egypt was among the fastest-growing economies in MENA. Tourism
recovered to near pre-revolution levels. The IMF program was completed.
However, poverty remained high and income inequality was significant.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2020.assessment:
Egypt was exceptional globally in achieving positive GDP growth
(3.6%) during COVID-19 in 2020. GDP per capita reached $3,547 in
nominal terms (matching 2015 peak). However, tourism collapsed
again with COVID-19, and underlying vulnerabilities persisted.
IMF Rapid Financing Instrument ($2.8 billion) was used.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2021.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to $3,699 in 2021 with real growth of 3.3%.
Egypt continued to grow faster than regional peers. However, underlying
vulnerabilities (high debt, import dependency, food insecurity) were
building. USD nominal figure rose due to pound stability. Inflation
moderated temporarily.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2022.assessment:
Egypt's GDP per capita faced severe pressures in 2022. The Ukraine
war disrupted wheat imports (Egypt is the world's largest wheat importer).
The pound devalued sharply (multiple steps from EGP 15 to EGP 24/USD).
GDP growth was positive (~6.6%) but economic crisis indicators were
rising with inflation reaching 18-21%. New IMF Extended Fund Facility
negotiations opened in 2022.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2023.assessment:
GDP per capita fell to approximately $3,503 in 2023 as Egypt faced
its worst economic crisis in decades. The pound depreciated from ~EGP 24
to ~EGP 31/USD. Inflation peaked at 38-40%. Growth slowed to ~3.8%.
The IMF Extended Fund Facility ($3 billion, December 2022) was being
implemented. Severe foreign exchange shortage. New Gulf investment deals
(notably the $35 billion Ras el-Hekma UAE deal in February 2024)
were being finalized.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

GDP per capita declined further in 2024 as the major currency

income. The $35 billion UAE Ras el-Hekma deal (February 2024) helped
stabilize foreign reserves. Real growth continued at ~2.5-4%.

1996.assessment:
Egypt's inflation was approximately 7.2% in 1996, declining from
higher levels in the early 1990s as the IMF structural adjustment
program achieved macroeconomic stabilization. Price stability was
improving. Food prices were the primary driver. Subsidy policies
helped buffer lower-income households.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt CPI inflation, accessed 2026-03-01

1997.assessment:
Inflation declined to approximately 6.2% in 1997 as the economy
stabilized. The Luxor massacre had little direct inflationary effect
though the tourism collapse had broader economic implications.
Monetary policy maintained relative price stability.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

1998.assessment:
Inflation fell to approximately 4.2% in 1998. Global commodity prices
were low (Asian financial crisis effects). Egypt maintained price
stability through a fixed exchange rate policy against the US dollar.
The Central Bank of Egypt maintained orthodox monetary policy.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

1999.assessment:
Inflation remained low at approximately 3.8% in 1999. Egypt maintained
price stability through the late 1990s. The fixed exchange rate policy
anchored inflation expectations.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2000.assessment:
Inflation fell to approximately 2.8% in 2000, below 3% for the
first time in years. The Egyptian economy was benefiting from low
global commodity prices and the fixed exchange rate anchor. Despite
currency pressure building, consumer prices were stable.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2001.assessment:
Inflation remained very low at approximately 2.3% in 2001. Despite
mounting currency pressure and the post-9/11 economic shock, consumer
price inflation was contained. The fixed exchange rate policy absorbed
external shocks at the cost of growing currency overvaluation.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2002.assessment:
Inflation remained low at approximately 2.7% in 2002. The pound was
under significant pressure with multiple mini-devaluations. Full float
came in January 2003. Consumer prices were artificially suppressed
by the fixed rate policy and subsidies.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2003.assessment:
Inflation rose to approximately 4.5% in 2003 following the January
2003 pound float, which caused a ~50% devaluation. Import prices
rose. The pass-through to consumer prices was moderate due to
continued subsidies on key commodities. The inflation rise was
more contained than expected given the devaluation magnitude.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2004.assessment:
Inflation rose sharply to approximately 11.3% in 2004 as the full
effects of the 2003 devaluation passed through to consumer prices.
Rising global commodity prices (oil, food) added to inflationary
pressure. This was the highest inflation since the early 1990s.
The Central Bank tightened monetary policy in response.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2005.assessment:
Inflation fell sharply to approximately 4.9% in 2005 as the
devaluation effects normalized and monetary policy tightened.
Egypt's strong economic growth (6-7%) did not produce significant
inflation in 2005. The pound was stable. Price stability was restored.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2006.assessment:
Inflation rose to approximately 7.6% in 2006 driven by global
commodity price increases, particularly food. Egypt's strong economic
growth also contributed to demand-pull inflation. Food subsidies
helped contain social impact but the fiscal cost was rising.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2007.assessment:
Inflation rose to approximately 9.3% in 2007. Global food price
increases were major contributors. Egypt's strong growth added demand
pressure. Food subsidies were strained. Social pressure over rising
food prices was building, contributing to April 2008 bread riots.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2008.assessment:
Inflation spiked to approximately 18.3% in 2008, the highest in
over a decade. Global food and energy price spikes were the primary
driver. Bread riots in Mahalla al-Kubra (April 2008) were a direct
response. The Egyptian government increased food subsidies as an
emergency measure. Significant social hardship resulted.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2009.assessment:
Inflation moderated to approximately 11.8% in 2009 as global food
and energy prices fell from their 2008 peaks due to the financial
crisis. Still elevated, reflecting structural price pressures in
Egypt. Food subsidies continued to buffer lower-income households.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2010.assessment:
Inflation remained elevated at approximately 11.7% in 2010. Food
price inflation was a key grievance contributing to the January 2011
revolution. Global food prices rose again in 2010 (Russian wheat
export ban, poor harvests). Egypt's structural food import dependence
made it vulnerable to global price shocks.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2011.assessment:
Inflation remained elevated at approximately 11.1% in 2011.
Revolutionary disruption to supply chains and distribution networks
added upward pressure. The SCAF government maintained food subsidies.
High food prices were a continuing social concern in the transition period.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2012.assessment:
Inflation moderated to approximately 7.1% in 2012 as global commodity
prices eased. The Morsi government maintained food subsidies. The
economic slowdown from political instability also reduced demand-pull
inflation. The moderation was welcome but food prices remained a
social concern.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2013.assessment:
Inflation rose to approximately 9.5% in 2013. Energy subsidy costs
were growing dramatically. Political instability (coup, Rabaa crackdown)
disrupted supply chains and investor confidence. Gulf state aid ($12B)
helped avoid a worse outcome. Energy prices pressured inflation.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2014.assessment:
Inflation rose to approximately 10.1% in 2014. Sisi's government
implemented a major energy subsidy cut (July 2014) reducing fuel and
electricity subsidies dramatically, which drove up prices. This was
a necessary reform but had an inflationary impact. IMF-encouraged
fiscal consolidation was underway.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2015.assessment:
Inflation was approximately 11.0% in 2015 as subsidy reductions
continued to pass through to prices. The economy was growing but
inflationary pressures from structural reforms (energy price reform)
were significant. The pound came under pressure as foreign reserves
declined.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2016.assessment:
Inflation rose to approximately 13.8% in 2016. The November 2016
pound float/devaluation (~50% against USD) caused an immediate
inflationary shock. IMF program conditionality required additional
subsidy reductions. The full inflationary impact of the devaluation
was felt most in 2017.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2017.assessment:
Inflation peaked at approximately 23.5% in 2017, the highest since
the early 1990s. The full-year impact of the 2016 pound devaluation
drove up import prices dramatically. Additional subsidy cuts under
the IMF program (electricity, fuel) added pressure. Significant
social hardship resulted with real wages falling sharply.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2018.assessment:
Inflation moderated to approximately 14.4% in 2018 as the pound
stabilized and monetary policy tightened under the IMF program.
Still elevated but the declining trend was positive. Additional
energy price reforms continued. The Central Bank raised interest
rates significantly to combat inflation.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2019.assessment:
Inflation fell significantly to approximately 9.2% in 2019 as the
IMF program achieved price stabilization. The pound was relatively
stable. Energy price reforms largely completed. The Central Bank
cut rates as inflation fell. Economic stabilization was visible.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2020.assessment:
Inflation fell to approximately 5.7% in 2020. COVID-19 reduced
demand and import prices fell (oil prices collapsed). The pound
was relatively stable. Egypt's central bank cut interest rates
aggressively to support growth during COVID-19. Price stability
was broadly maintained.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2021.assessment:
Inflation remained relatively moderate at approximately 5.2% in 2021.
Egypt was managing post-COVID recovery. Supply chain pressures globally
were building but Egypt's unique combination of capital controls and
monetary policy somewhat muted the impact. Currency was relatively stable.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2022.assessment:
Inflation rose sharply to approximately 13.9% in 2022. The Ukraine
war caused global food and energy price spikes, devastating Egypt
as the world's largest wheat importer. The pound devalued multiple
times (EGP 15 → EGP 19 → EGP 24/USD). Global supply chain shocks
compounded the food price crisis.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2023.assessment:
Inflation reached approximately 33.9% in 2023, the highest since
the 1986 debt crisis era. The pound devalued further (EGP 24 → EGP 31/USD)
and import prices surged. Food inflation reached ~70% peak in some
categories. The IMF Extended Fund Facility ($3 billion) required
exchange rate flexibility. Severe cost-of-living crisis for ordinary Egyptians.

sources.citation: IMF WEO Database, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2024.assessment:
Inflation remained very elevated in 2024 at approximately 28%, but
declined from the 2023 peak following the major March 2024 devaluation
(EGP 31 → ~EGP 50/USD, -38%) and IMF program implementation. Monetary
policy tightening continued. The UAE Ras el-Hekma deal helped stabilize
the external position. Inflation was on a declining trajectory.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook April 2025, Egypt

2025.assessment:
Inflation expected to continue declining in 2025 from 2023-2024 peaks
as monetary tightening takes effect and the exchange rate stabilizes
at the new lower level. IMF projections suggest inflation falling to
12-15% range by end of 2025.

1996.assessment:
Egypt's trade openness (exports + imports as % of GDP) was approximately
44.2% in 1996. A moderately open economy with significant import
dependence. Exports were led by oil/gas, Suez Canal revenues (counted
in services), and tourism. The Egyptian pound was pegged, maintaining
stable trade patterns.

Trade openness remained at approximately 44.5% in 1997. Relatively
stable. Luxor massacre reduced tourism (a major export service) but
other trade was unaffected. Suez Canal revenues continued steady.

1998.assessment:
Trade openness approximately 42% in 1998. The economy had
reasonable international trade integration. Imports were dominant
(Egypt is a net importer). Oil revenues provided export capacity.
Trade was somewhat restricted by the fixed exchange rate.

1999.assessment:
Trade openness approximately 43.5% in 1999. Moderately open economy.

and Arab states. The economy was growing with stable international
trade flows.

Trade openness approximately 44.8% in 2000. EU-Egypt Association
Agreement signed in 2001. The economy maintained moderate trade

industrial goods.

Trade openness fell slightly to approximately 41% in 2001.

Trade openness approximately 40% in 2002. The economy was under

Trade openness rose to approximately 44.3% in 2003 after the

GAFTA (Arab free trade area) membership.

Trade openness approximately 46.7% in 2004. The economy was opening

Trade openness rose to approximately 53.6% in 2005. Egypt was
becoming more internationally integrated during the reform period.
Gas exports expanded. FDI-driven imports increased. Tourism revenue
from services trade grew strongly. The economy was more open than
a decade earlier.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, trade openness, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

Trade openness at approximately 58.8% in 2006. Egypt was approaching

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, trade openness, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2007.assessment:
Trade openness approximately 60.3% in 2007, the peak of Egypt's

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, trade openness, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

2008.assessment:
Trade openness peaked at approximately 68.3% in 2008. High global
commodity prices inflated both the export and import sides. The
investment boom required massive imports of capital goods. Energy
export revenues were at their highest. This was the peak of Egypt's
international economic integration.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, trade openness, Egypt, accessed 2026-03-01

Trade openness fell to approximately 53.5% in 2009 as global commodity
prices collapsed during the financial crisis. Oil export revenues fell
sharply. Import volumes also declined with the economic slowdown.
The reduction was driven by price effects more than volume changes.

Trade openness approximately 54.8% in 2010 as commodity prices

Trade openness approximately 53.3% in 2011. Tourism services exports

services export (tourism) component fell.

Trade openness approximately 50.8% in 2013. Political instability

Egypt's official unemployment was approximately 8.8% in 1996.
Structural unemployment was a chronic feature of Egypt's economy
with a large public sector absorbing graduate unemployment. Youth
unemployment was significantly higher than the official rate.
Informal employment was substantial.

1996.assessment:
Egypt maintained deep multilateral integration in 1996. Founding member of the
Arab League (1945, Cairo HQ), African Union (formerly OAU), Non-Aligned Movement,
Organisation of Islamic Cooperation. Full UN member with active participation.
Camp David Accords (1979) normalized relations with Israel, making Egypt unique
among Arab states. Egypt chaired the AU in the 1990s. Active WTO member.
Full diplomatic relations with over 150 countries. Egypt hosted Arab League
headquarters in Cairo.

sources.citation: UN Member States list
Arab League Charter and membership records

1997.assessment:
Full multilateral integration maintained. Egypt played active mediation role in
Israeli-Palestinian process. Relations with most Arab states normal. Egypt's
mediation role in regional conflicts reinforced its diplomatic standing.

sources.citation: UN records

1998.assessment:
Full multilateral integration. Egypt served on UN Security Council as non-permanent
member 1996-1997. Active in all major multilateral forums. Relations with most
countries normalized. Egypt's strategic position (Suez Canal, Israel border,
Arab world's most populous state) made it a key diplomatic actor.

sources.citation: UN Security Council historical membership records

1999.assessment:
Full multilateral integration. Egypt continued active diplomatic role in
Israeli-Palestinian peace process (Oslo follow-up, Camp David 2 preparations).
Egypt maintained normal relations with all major powers. Full participation
in UN, WTO, IMF, World Bank institutions.

sources.citation: UN records

2000.assessment:
Full multilateral integration. Camp David II summit (July 2000, US-hosted) failed
to resolve Israeli-Palestinian issues; Second Intifada began September 2000.
Egypt's mediation role intensified. Egypt maintained normalized relations with
Israel despite Arab League pressure following Intifada, making it a crucial
intermediary. Relations with US at strong bilateral level.

sources.citation: US State Department records on Camp David II

2001.assessment:
Full multilateral integration deepened post-9/11. Egypt became a key member of
the US-led global counterterrorism coalition. Egypt joined the Global Coalition
Against Terrorism framework. Relations with Western powers strengthened. Some
tension with Arab civil society over Egyptian government's use of anti-terror
laws to suppress domestic dissent.

sources.citation: US State Department

2002.assessment:
Full multilateral integration. Egypt played important role in Arab League Arab
Peace Initiative (Beirut Summit, March 2002). Egypt endorsed the initiative
offering Israel normalized relations in exchange for Palestinian state. Egypt
maintained its unique position as Arab state with normalized Israeli relations.

sources.citation: Arab League Beirut Summit, Arab Peace Initiative, March 2002

2003.assessment:
Full multilateral integration. Egypt publicly opposed Iraq War but did not break
ties with US-led coalition members. Egypt maintained Suez Canal transit rights
for US vessels. Full participation in all multilateral forums. Egypt's opposition
to the Iraq War was expressed through diplomatic channels without reducing its
overall integration.

sources.citation: UN records on Iraq War diplomacy

2004.assessment:
Full multilateral integration. EU-Egypt Association Agreement entered into force
(June 2004), creating a free trade framework and strengthening EU-Egypt diplomatic
ties. Egypt participated in EU Neighbourhood Policy framework. Full WTO membership.
Relations with Africa deepened through AU participation.

sources.citation: EU-Egypt Association Agreement, OJ L304, June 2004

2005.assessment:
Full multilateral integration with slight diplomatic tensions from democracy
rhetoric. Condoleezza Rice's Cairo speech (June 2005) represented the height of
US democracy promotion pressure, but the strategic relationship remained intact.
Egypt maintained full multilateral participation. Egypt hosted parliamentary
elections that international observers criticized as fraudulent.

sources.citation: EU Neighbourhood Policy Egypt Action Plan

2006.assessment:
Full multilateral integration. Egypt played a key diplomatic role during the
Israel-Lebanon (Hezbollah) War (July-August 2006), supporting ceasefire efforts.
Egypt's position as a moderate Arab voice strengthened its diplomatic standing.
Relations with Hamas-led Palestinian Authority strained but Gaza mediation role
maintained.

sources.citation: UN Security Council records on Lebanon 2006

2007.assessment:
Full multilateral integration. Egypt hosted Arab League Summit. Mediated Hamas-
Fatah conflict (Mecca Agreement). Full participation in WTO, IMF, World Bank,
AU, OIC. Egypt's diplomatic activity was at a high point, leveraging its position
as regional intermediary.

sources.citation: Arab League Summit records 2007

2008.assessment:
Full multilateral integration. Egypt mediated Gaza ceasefire (June 2008 Hamas-Israel
ceasefire, Egyptian brokered). Full participation in all multilateral forums.
Egypt's economy attracted significant FDI from Gulf, Europe, and US, reinforcing
international engagement. No diplomatic isolation of any kind.

sources.citation: UN records; Egyptian Foreign Ministry statements

2009.assessment:
Full multilateral integration. Obama Cairo speech (June 2009) reinforced US-Egypt
engagement. Egypt served as key partner in US Middle East policy. Relations with
Turkey under Erdogan beginning to diverge on Gaza/Hamas issues but remained
formally correct. Full AU, Arab League, UN participation.

sources.citation: Obama Cairo Speech, White House, June 2009

2010.assessment:
Full multilateral integration maintained. Egypt's fraudulent November 2010
elections generated international criticism but no diplomatic consequences.
Egypt continued active participation in regional mediation. Relations with
key partners (US, EU, Arab Gulf states, Israel) normal. No diplomatic isolation.

sources.citation: UN records

2011.assessment:
Diplomatic integration maintained through revolutionary transition. No countries
severed relations with Egypt during revolution. SCAF was quickly recognized by
international community. US, EU, Arab League all maintained normal relations
with SCAF-led transitional government. Some deterioration in relations with
Israel as Egyptian protesters attacked Israeli embassy (September 2011).
Egypt withdrew ambassador from Israel temporarily but did not abrogate Camp David.

sources.citation: US State Department statements on Egypt transition 2011
Human Rights Watch, Egypt: Protect Protesters

2012.assessment:
Full multilateral integration continued under Morsi. Morsi's government engaged
internationally — visiting China, Iran (first Egyptian president to visit since
1979), Turkey, and Gulf states. Egypt assumed non-permanent UN Security Council
seat (2016-17 term approved). Relations with Iran's resumption raised some concern
in Gulf but did not reduce overall integration.

sources.citation: UN General Assembly records 2012

2013.assessment:
Diplomatic integration maintained post-coup. Gulf states (Saudi Arabia, UAE,
Kuwait) immediately backed Sisi, providing $12 billion in aid. This divided
Arab world — Qatar and Turkey condemned the coup and backed Morsi/MB. Egypt
recalled its ambassador from Qatar. Turkey-Egypt relations ruptured. However,
Egypt maintained full Western relations — the US and EU did not impose full
diplomatic isolation despite condemning the coup.

sources.citation: Arab League records on Egypt 2013
Reuters, Egypt-Qatar diplomatic rupture 2013

2014.assessment:
Diplomatic integration largely restored following Sisi's presidential election
(May 2014). Western governments normalized relations with Sisi government. Turkey
and Qatar remained hostile. Egypt expelled Qatari-funded Al Jazeera journalists.
Egypt designated the Muslim Brotherhood as a terrorist organization (December 2013),
creating ongoing friction with Qatar/Turkey which hosted MB figures.

sources.citation: US State Department Egypt Relations

2015.assessment:
Diplomatic integration improved. Sharm el-Sheikh investment conference (March 2015)
was a major diplomatic success, attracting 37 heads of state and government.
US fully restored military relationship. EU Association Agreement remained active.
Egypt joined Saudi-led intervention in Yemen (March 2015), reinforcing Gulf
coalition membership. Turkey-Qatar-Egypt rupture continued.

sources.citation: Egypt Economic Development Conference, Sharm el-Sheikh, 2015

2016.assessment:
Diplomatic integration maintained but strained. Giulio Regeni murder (January
2016, Italian PhD student) caused severe Italian-Egyptian diplomatic crisis; Italy
recalled ambassador for 18 months. EU Parliament condemned Egypt. However, EU-
Egypt Association remained in force. IMF negotiations concluded $12 billion deal
(November 2016). Turkey-Qatar-Egypt rupture continued.

sources.citation: Italian Ministry of Foreign Affairs on Regeni case

2017.assessment:
Diplomatic integration continued with ongoing Turkey-Qatar tensions. The June 2017
Qatar blockade by Saudi Arabia, UAE, Bahrain, and Egypt represented Egypt joining
a major regional diplomatic rupture. Egypt cut diplomatic ties with Qatar (June 2017).
This was the most significant diplomatic event of the year, placing Egypt firmly
in the anti-Qatar bloc. Italy still without ambassador (Regeni).

sources.citation: Egypt cuts ties with Qatar, Al Jazeera, June 5 2017

2018.assessment:
Egypt-Qatar rift continued. Turkey-Egypt relations remained severed. EU Association
Agreement active. US-Egypt military relationship fully restored under Trump.
Egypt participated in UN Security Council as non-permanent member (2016-2017 term).
IMF program continuing. Italy-Egypt relations improving but Regeni case unresolved.

sources.citation: US Department of State Egypt Relations

2019.assessment:
Qatar blockade continued. Turkey-Egypt relations still ruptured. Egypt attended
Arab League and AU summits. Full participation in multilateral forums. IMF program
completed, improving Egypt's standing with international financial institutions.
Egypt became chair of AU (2019). Regeni case continued to strain Italy relations.

sources.citation: African Union Egypt Chairmanship 2019

2020.assessment:
Qatar blockade continued until January 2021. Turkey-Egypt relations improved
slightly as both contested Libya conflict (Egypt backed Haftar, Turkey backed GNA).
Libya competition created direct Egypt-Turkey tensions. IMF COVID-19 assistance
demonstrated full integration with international financial system. Italy returned
ambassador to Cairo (May 2021 per later timeline; 2020 still without ambassador).

sources.citation: IMF COVID-19 Emergency Financing for Egypt

2021.assessment:
Al-Ula Declaration (January 2021) ended Qatar blockade — Egypt and Qatar resumed
diplomatic relations. Italy returned ambassador to Cairo (May 2021) with Egyptian
commitment to Regeni prosecution. Turkey-Egypt diplomatic normalization process began
with intelligence contacts and diplomatic meetings. Egypt's overall diplomatic
integration improved significantly this year.

sources.citation: Al-Ula Declaration, GCC Summit, January 2021
Italy returns ambassador to Egypt, May 2021

2022.assessment:
Full multilateral integration largely restored. Egypt-Qatar relations normalized
following Al-Ula. Turkey-Egypt normalization advanced with Sisi-Erdogan handshake
at FIFA World Cup (November 2022 — first high-level contact since 2013). Egypt's
balanced stance on Russia-Ukraine (UNGA abstention) reflected strategic autonomy
rather than isolation. IMF $3 billion EFF agreed December 2022.

sources.citation: Sisi-Erdogan handshake at Qatar World Cup, November 2022

2023.assessment:
Full multilateral integration. Turkey-Egypt relations normalized with reciprocal
ambassador exchanges (July 2023). Egypt-Qatar relations fully normal. Egypt's
central role in Gaza ceasefire mediation (post-October 7 Hamas attack) elevated
diplomatic standing with Western and Arab partners. Egypt joined BRICS (invited
January 2024, effective date). Egypt-Saudi Arabia-US mediation trio for Gaza.

sources.citation: Egypt-Turkey ambassadors restored, July 2023
Egypt invited to join BRICS, August 2023

2024.assessment:
Full multilateral integration. Egypt formally joined BRICS (January 2024).
Egypt's Gaza mediation role continued, with Egypt-Qatar-US troika central to
ceasefire negotiations. IMF $8 billion EFF (March 2024). UAE Ras el-Hekma deal
($35 billion). Egypt's diplomatic integration at its broadest since the 2011 era.
Red Sea crisis (Houthi attacks) reduced Suez revenues, creating economic diplomatic pressure.

sources.citation: Egypt joins BRICS, January 1 2024

2025.assessment:
Full multilateral integration maintained. Egypt continues as BRICS member,
AU member, Arab League member, UN full participant. Gaza mediation role ongoing.
US-Egypt bilateral relationship strong. IMF program continuing. Egypt's
diplomatic integration is at its most comprehensive and balanced in decades.

sources.citation: UN records

1996.assessment:
Egypt attracted modest FDI of approximately 0.9% of GDP in 1996. Investment was
primarily in oil and gas sector exploration and some manufacturing. The Investment
Guarantee and Protection Agreement with the US and EU bilateral investment treaties
provided a legal framework but the investment climate was constrained by bureaucracy,
Exchange rate controls, and limited business environment reforms.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FDI net inflows

1997.assessment:
Modest increase in FDI. Post-Luxor massacre (November 1997) had some chilling
effect on tourism-related investment. However, natural gas sector continued
attracting international oil companies. Egypt-EU Association Agreement
negotiations advanced, improving investor outlook.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

1998.assessment:
FDI remained modest. Asian financial crisis reduced global FDI flows.
Egypt's oil and gas sector continued to attract international investment.
Tourism sector recovery after Luxor massacre gradual. Business environment
improvements were slow.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

1999.assessment:
Stable low FDI. Privatization program attracted some foreign investment.
Real estate and manufacturing sectors beginning to see increased interest.
Egypt's FDI remained below regional comparators in this period.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2000.assessment:
FDI remained modest despite privatization program. Second Intifada (beginning
September 2000) created regional uncertainty. Natural gas sector new discoveries
attracted majors (BP, Shell, BG Group). Egypt's favorable geology and proximity
to European gas markets drove sustained oil/gas FDI despite political risks.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2001.assessment:
FDI fell sharply following 9/11 attacks, global recession, and regional security
concerns. Tourist sector investment stalled. Currency pressures ahead of
devaluation reduced investor confidence. Egypt was one of the most affected MENA
states in terms of FDI decline in 2001-2003.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2002.assessment:
FDI remained depressed. Iraq War buildup created regional uncertainty. Currency
black market and exchange rate instability deterred investment. Egypt's FDI
at its lowest point since the early 1990s.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2003.assessment:
FDI at trough — approximately 0.4% of GDP. Iraq War (March 2003), currency float
(January 2003), and regional insecurity combined to keep FDI minimal. Post-float,
however, Egypt's improved exchange rate transparency began to attract interest.
Nazif reform government (appointed July 2004) would reverse this trend from 2004.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2004.assessment:
FDI began recovering with Nazif government economic reforms (tax reform, customs
reduction, privatization). EU-Egypt Association Agreement entered into force (June
2004), improving EU investor confidence. Natural gas sector continued attracting
international oil companies. Recovery trajectory firmly established.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2005.assessment:
FDI surged to 3.5% of GDP — highest in over a decade. Reforms under Nazif cabinet
(privatization, banking consolidation, flat 20% income tax) dramatically improved
investor sentiment. Arab Gulf investors, particularly from UAE, Saudi Arabia, and
Kuwait, drove real estate and telecom FDI surge. LNG export facilities attracted
international investment. Egypt became one of Africa/MENA's top FDI destinations.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI
UNCTAD World Investment Report 2006

2006.assessment:
FDI peaked at nearly 6% of GDP. Egypt's largest-ever FDI year to this point.
Gulf sovereign wealth funds invested heavily in real estate (especially New Cairo,
coastal developments). Major transactions included privatization of Banque du
Caire, Sidi Kerir Petrochemicals, and other state assets. Telecom sector attracted
major investments. Egypt listed as top 10 FDI destination in Africa.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI
UNCTAD World Investment Report 2007

2007.assessment:
FDI reached all-time high of approximately 8% of GDP. Egypt was the largest FDI
recipient in Africa and the Arab world in 2007. Investments driven by Gulf real
estate boom spilling into Egypt, natural gas export infrastructure, and telecom
expansion. Vodafone and other international telecom firms expanded. Egypt's
economic boom period peaked in 2007-2008.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI; UNCTAD WIR 2008

2008.assessment:
FDI remained high at nearly 6% of GDP despite global financial crisis. Natural
gas sector investment continued. Real estate cooling slightly. Food price crisis
(bread riots April 2008) raised political risk concern but did not immediately
deter investment. Global financial crisis impact felt more in 2009.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2009.assessment:
FDI fell substantially as global financial crisis reduced global capital flows
and Gulf states cut back on investment. Real estate construction projects were
cancelled or delayed. However, Egypt's oil and gas sector maintained investment
flows. FDI remained above pre-2005 levels, reflecting structural improvement.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2010.assessment:
FDI stabilized at approximately 3% of GDP. Partial recovery from 2009 decline.
Natural gas sector investment continued. Political tensions ahead of fraudulent
November 2010 elections created some investor uncertainty. Real estate sector
activity recovering. Egypt's pre-revolution FDI remained robust compared to
1996-2004 levels.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2011.assessment:
FDI collapsed to approximately 0.5% of GDP — lowest since 2002. Revolution (January-
February 2011) created severe political risk; international investors evacuated
staff and halted new commitments. Capital flight estimated at $6-8 billion.
Tourism FDI ceased. Real estate construction halted. Only oil/gas sector
maintained any continued investment. Country risk assessments elevated Egypt
to highest risk categories.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI; UNCTAD WIR 2012

2012.assessment:
Modest FDI recovery under Morsi government. Investor uncertainty persisted due
to political instability, SCAF-Morsi tensions, and constitutional crisis
(November 2012). Oil and gas investment partially recovered. Gulf investors
cautious given Muslim Brotherhood government. Qatar invested approximately $2.5
billion in bonds/deposits (not counted as FDI) to support reserves.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2013.assessment:
FDI remained subdued despite post-coup Gulf financial support. Saudi Arabia, UAE,
and Kuwait pledged $12 billion in aid (mostly deposits and grants, not equity FDI).
Rabaa massacre (August 2013) and ongoing political violence deterred portfolio
investors. Oil and gas sector FDI continued. Investor confidence remained low
pending political stabilization.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2014.assessment:
Modest FDI recovery following Sisi's election (May 2014) and improved political
stability. Oil and gas sector continued attracting investment. Gulf real estate
development projects restarted in some Egyptian coastal areas. FMF subsidy reform
(July 2014) improved investor confidence in fiscal management. Still well below
pre-revolution levels.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2015.assessment:
FDI recovered to approximately 2.7% of GDP. Sharm el-Sheikh investment conference
(March 2015) attracted $36 billion in pledges and boosted investor sentiment.
ENI announced Zohr gas field discovery (August 2015) — largest gas field in
Mediterranean, attracting major investment. Gulf investors restarted mega-projects.
Metrojet bombing (October 2015) created new setback for tourism-related FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI; ENI Zohr field announcement August 2015

2016.assessment:
FDI increased to 3.4% driven primarily by ENI Zohr gas field development investment.
Zohr investment commitments alone represented multi-billion dollar upstream FDI.
November 2016 IMF deal and pound float improved macroeconomic credibility for
longer-term investors. However, immediate post-devaluation period saw some
investor caution due to inflation shock.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI; ENI Egypt investment records

2017.assessment:
FDI rose sharply to 4.7% of GDP, highest since pre-revolution, driven by Zohr
gas field development and IMF program credibility. Egypt's IMF reform program
attracted international capital. Gulf sovereign funds resumed investment. BP,
ENI, and other majors accelerated commitments. High inflation (~23%) paradoxically
reduced cost of local-currency inputs for foreign investors.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI; UNCTAD WIR 2018

2018.assessment:
Strong FDI maintained above 4% of GDP. Zohr field production began (December 2017),
validating the investment case. Egypt became a net gas exporter by 2018. Continued
oil and gas sector investment. Gulf sovereign investment maintained. IMF program
on track boosted investor confidence. Egypt improved in Doing Business rankings.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2019.assessment:
FDI moderated as Zohr development phase peaked. Oil and gas sector FDI remained
the dominant component. IMF program completion (July 2019) maintained investor
confidence. Egypt GDP growth reached 5.6% (best since 2008), reinforcing
positive investment climate. New Investment Law (2017) and streamlined procedures
beginning to show results.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2020.assessment:
FDI fell sharply due to COVID-19 pandemic. Global FDI collapsed by over 40%
in 2020. Tourism-related investment ceased. Oil and gas investment cut back
as oil prices fell. However, Egypt's relatively resilient GDP (3.6% growth
vs global recession) cushioned the decline. New Administrative Capital construction
attracted some domestic and Gulf investment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI; UNCTAD WIR 2021

2021.assessment:
FDI recovery to 2.4% of GDP. Oil and gas investment recovering with oil price
rise. Gulf sovereign investment in New Administrative Capital and real estate.
Tourism sector FDI still constrained. Egypt attracted green hydrogen investment
interest and renewable energy projects (Benban solar park operational).

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2022.assessment:
FDI moderated. Ukraine war created uncertainty and portfolio outflows (~$20 billion)
from Egyptian treasury bills. Three currency devaluations in 2022 created
uncertainty for FDI planners. Oil and gas sector maintained investment. Gulf
investors cautious ahead of IMF deal. Year ended with $3 billion IMF EFF agreed
December 2022.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI

2023.assessment:
FDI remained subdued amid ongoing currency uncertainty and high inflation (33.9%).
IMF program required structural reforms affecting investment climate. Gaza war
(October 2023) created regional risk premium. Oil and gas sector provided
baseline FDI. UAE interest in Egyptian real estate and infrastructure maintained
some investment flow.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI preliminary 2023 data

2024.assessment:
UAE Ras el-Hekma deal ($35 billion announced February 2024) represented the
largest FDI commitment in Egyptian history, though structured partly as land
sale and investment over multiple years rather than immediate FDI inflow.
March 2024 pound float and IMF $8 billion deal improved investor confidence.
FDI recovery expected as macroeconomic stabilization advances.

sources.citation: UAE Ras el-Hekma investment deal, February 2024

2025.assessment:
FDI outlook positive given IMF program, Ras el-Hekma implementation, and
improving macroeconomic stability. Red Sea shipping crisis (Houthi attacks)
reduces Suez revenues but does not directly affect FDI inflows. Egypt's
renewable energy and green hydrogen sectors attracting increased interest.
Data not yet available.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook projections

1996.assessment:
Egypt received approximately $1.3 billion in annual US Foreign Military Financing
(FMF) in 1996, one of the largest recipients globally after Israel. This stemmed
from the Camp David Accords (1978) framework. US military equipment included F-16
fighters, M1A1 Abrams tanks (co-produced in Egypt), Apache helicopters, and naval
vessels. Egypt also maintained defense cooperation with France (Mirage jets),
Germany, and UK. No foreign military presence on Egyptian soil (US withdrew Sinai
observer forces from MFO but Multinational Force and Observers remained).

sources.citation: US Defense Security Cooperation Agency, Historical FMF Data
Congressional Research Service, US Military Aid to Egypt

1997.assessment:
US FMF continued at $1.3 billion. Egypt-US military cooperation deepened with
annual Bright Star exercises (largest military exercise in MENA region). M1A1
Abrams tank co-production in Egypt continued at Factory 200, Helwan. Egypt
purchased additional F-16 Block 40 aircraft under Peace Vector program.

sources.citation: US DSCA Arms Transfer Notifications

1998.assessment:
Continued $1.3 billion US FMF. Egypt-US military relationship remained robust.
Egypt participated in NATO Partnership for Peace (observer status). France and
other European defense suppliers maintained contracts for spare parts and systems.
No foreign military bases on Egyptian territory beyond UN Multinational Force
and Observers (Sinai monitoring body).

sources.citation: US Defense Security Cooperation Agency

1999.assessment:
$1.3 billion US FMF. Egypt-US Bright Star exercise held with record participation.
Continued M1A1 tank co-production. Egypt's military modernization funded almost
entirely through US FMF program, making it heavily dependent on US military
supply chain and spare parts.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense, Bright Star exercise records

2000.assessment:
Continued $1.3 billion US FMF. Second Intifada began September 2000; Egyptian
military cooperation with Israel on border security continued under Camp David
framework. Multinational Force and Observers (MFO) Sinai mission maintained
with US, French, and other contingents.

sources.citation: US DSCA annual report 2000

2001.assessment:
Post-9/11 deepened Egypt-US military cooperation. Egypt provided intelligence
and participated in counterterrorism cooperation. US FMF maintained at $1.3
billion. Egypt facilitated US military logistics through Suez Canal and allowed
overflight rights for US aircraft. CIA-Egypt intelligence cooperation on
extraordinary rendition program intensified.

sources.citation: US DSCA; Senate Intelligence Committee Report on Renditions

2002.assessment:
$1.3 billion US FMF. Egypt-US counterterrorism cooperation remained the
cornerstone of the military relationship. Continued M1A1 co-production and
F-16 maintenance cooperation. Egypt's military received ongoing US training
through International Military Education and Training (IMET) programs.

sources.citation: US DSCA annual report

2003.assessment:
$1.3 billion US FMF maintained despite Egypt opposing Iraq War. Egypt facilitated
US naval transit through Suez Canal even while publicly opposing the invasion.
This demonstrated the strategic nature of the relationship beyond political alignment.
Bright Star 2003 exercise conducted.

sources.citation: US DSCA; Suez Canal Authority transit records

2004.assessment:
Continued $1.3 billion US FMF. Sinai bombings (Taba, October 2004) led to
increased security cooperation in Sinai with intelligence sharing. Egypt deepened
cooperation with EU security services. France concluded additional defense sales.
Egypt's military remained overwhelmingly US-equipped for major platforms.

sources.citation: US DSCA annual report 2004

2005.assessment:
$1.3 billion US FMF. Following Sharm el-Sheikh bombings (July 2005), Egypt-US
counterterrorism cooperation intensified specifically on Sinai security. Israel
withdrew from Gaza (August 2005) and Egypt took responsibility for Rafah crossing
management, deepening Egypt-EU (EUBAM Rafah) security cooperation.

sources.citation: EU Border Assistance Mission Rafah (EUBAM), established 2005

2006.assessment:
$1.3 billion US FMF. Dahab bombings (April 2006) led to further security
cooperation. Egypt supplied peacekeepers to UN UNIFIL mission in Lebanon
following Israel-Hezbollah war. Egypt's military-to-military relationship
with the US included annual Bright Star exercises with combined forces from
multiple US commands.

sources.citation: UN UNIFIL troop contribution records

2007.assessment:
$1.3 billion US FMF. F-16 Block 52 sale approved (additional 20 aircraft).
Egypt requested and received US approval for additional Abrams tanks. Continued
bilateral military exercises. Egypt's military budget increased substantially
during the 2000s boom years, supplementing US FMF with domestic defense spending.

sources.citation: US DSCA Notification, F-16 Block 52 for Egypt

2008.assessment:
$1.3 billion US FMF. Following Israeli-Hamas conflict and Egyptian-brokered
ceasefire (June 2008), Egypt's Sinai security role intensified. Egypt operated
anti-smuggling operations along Gaza border. Tunnels under Rafah were a source
of contention with Israel, leading to US pressure on Egypt to increase military
enforcement.

sources.citation: US DSCA annual report 2008

2009.assessment:
$1.3 billion US FMF. Operation Cast Lead aftermath (Israeli assault on Gaza,
Dec 2008-Jan 2009) increased Egypt's Sinai security responsibilities. Egypt
signed additional Memoranda of Understanding on counterterrorism with US and
EU partners. AH-64D Apache helicopter sale to Egypt approved.

sources.citation: US DSCA Apache sale notification for Egypt

2010.assessment:
$1.3 billion US FMF. Egypt's military received US training, equipment and
exercises throughout the Mubarak period. F-16 Peace Vector VI deliveries ongoing.
Egyptian military heavily US-integrated in terms of doctrine, training, and
equipment. This integration was a key factor in the military's eventual decision
to facilitate Mubarak's removal in 2011.

sources.citation: US DSCA annual report 2010

2011.assessment:
US FMF maintained at $1.3 billion through revolutionary transition. US Chairman
of the Joint Chiefs Admiral Mullen maintained direct communication with Egyptian
military chief General Tantawi throughout the revolution. The deep US-Egypt
military relationship was a critical factor in managing the transition — the
US urged Egyptian military not to fire on protesters. SCAF received uninterrupted
US military assistance. No suspension of aid or exercises.

sources.citation: Pentagon statements on Egypt military ties, January-February 2011
Congressional Research Service, Egypt Military Aid 2011

2012.assessment:
US FMF continued at $1.3 billion under Morsi presidency. Congress attached
conditions to some military aid citing uncertainty about civilian control of
military and democratic progress. US approved continued F-16 deliveries and
M1A1 co-production. Egypt-US military relationship survived the political
transition more intact than the civilian relationship.

sources.citation: Congressional Research Service, Egypt: Background and US Relations, 2013

2013.assessment:
Post-coup, US temporarily withheld $260 million in cash military aid and froze
delivery of Apache helicopters (October 2013), as well as F-16s and M1A1 tanks.
This partial suspension reflected US legal restrictions on aid to coup governments
but the US avoided formally declaring a coup. Gulf states (Saudi Arabia, UAE,
Kuwait) pledged $12 billion to offset reduced US financial flows. Egypt
accelerated diversification: purchased S-300 air defense systems from Russia
(confirmed 2014) and began diversifying away from sole US dependence.

sources.citation: US withholds military aid to Egypt, Reuters, October 2013
Congressional Research Service Egypt Military Aid 2013

2014.assessment:
US restored most military assistance following Sisi's presidential election
(May 2014). Apache helicopters delivered June 2014. F-16 deliveries resumed.
FMF restored to approximately $1.0-1.3 billion. Egypt simultaneously advanced
military diversification: France Mistral helicopter carrier deal (originally for
Russia, Egypt purchased instead in 2015), Russian S-300 discussions, Chinese
military equipment interests.

sources.citation: US Apache helicopter delivery to Egypt, June 2014

2015.assessment:
Egypt purchased two French Mistral helicopter carriers (originally built for Russia,
contract transferred to Egypt after Russia-Ukraine Crimea sanctions). First major
non-US capital defense purchase in decades. Egypt also signed Rafale fighter jet
deal with France (16 aircraft, $5.2 billion, February 2015) — first Rafale export
sale. Egypt joined Saudi-led Yemen coalition (March 2015) contributing naval forces.
US FMF at $1.3 billion.

sources.citation: France sells Rafale jets to Egypt, February 2015
Egypt purchases Mistral carriers from France

2016.assessment:
Egypt deepened military diversification. Additional Rafale purchase (24 more aircraft,
September 2016). Russian MiG-29M purchase discussions. Egypt received French FREMM
frigates. US FMF continued at $1.3 billion with some human rights conditionality
on $65.7M. Egypt's military was receiving equipment from US, France, Russia, and
Germany simultaneously, reflecting strategic diversification.

sources.citation: Egypt additional Rafale purchase 2016

2017.assessment:
US FMF at $1.3 billion minus $65.7M withheld for human rights (August 2017).
Egypt continued military procurement diversification. Operation Sinai ongoing
with intensified military operations against ISIS-Sinai Province. Egypt accepted
US military assistance for Sinai operations including intelligence sharing and
drone support.

sources.citation: US DSCA Egypt FMF records 2017

2018.assessment:
Full US FMF at $1.3 billion restored under Trump (who dropped human rights
conditions). Trump praised Sisi and Egypt-US military relationship strengthened.
Operation Sinai 2018 (February 2018) launched with US intelligence and logistics
support, significantly degrading ISIS-Sinai Province. Egypt received 24 F-16
Block 52 fighters, additional M1A1 tanks, and continued Apache deliveries.

sources.citation: US DSCA Egypt FMF 2018; Operation Sinai 2018

2019.assessment:
$1.3 billion US FMF. Egypt-US military relationship at its strongest since
pre-2011 period. Bright Star exercise resumed. Egypt also maintained French
military cooperation (additional Rafale deliveries). Russia-Egypt arms discussions
continued (potential SU-35 purchase was controversial with US due to CAATSA
concerns). US warned Egypt against SU-35 Russian purchase.

sources.citation: US DSCA Egypt FMF; CAATSA warnings to Egypt on SU-35

2020.assessment:
$1.3 billion US FMF. Egypt involved in Libya conflict (backing Haftar forces
against Turkey-supported GNA). Turkish-Egyptian military proxy confrontation in
Libya created the most direct Egypt-Turkey tensions since 2013 coup. Egypt
threatened direct military intervention in Libya. US provided intelligence
support for counter-ISIS operations in Sinai.

sources.citation: US DSCA Egypt 2020; Libya conflict reporting

2021.assessment:
Biden administration maintained $1.3 billion FMF but withheld $130 million
for human rights conditions (September 2021). Egypt received approval for
additional F-16 upgrades and M1A1 tank deliveries. Libya ceasefire negotiations
reduced Egypt-Turkey military tensions. Sinai insurgency continued declining.

sources.citation: Biden withholds $130M Egypt military aid, September 2021

2022.assessment:
US FMF at $1.3 billion (partial human rights withholding continued). Following
Russia's invasion of Ukraine, US focused on preventing Egypt from purchasing
Russian military equipment and maintaining Egypt in the Western-aligned defense
framework. Egypt's Russia balancing (UNGA abstentions) raised US concerns but
did not affect military aid. Egypt continued French Rafale deliveries.

sources.citation: US DSCA Egypt 2022

2023.assessment:
$1.3 billion US FMF. Gaza conflict (October 2023) brought Egypt-US-Israel military
coordination to the forefront. Egypt monitored Rafah crossing, coordinated with
US and Israel on hostage releases and aid delivery. Egypt maintained Sinai
demilitarization framework under Camp David but deployed additional forces near
Gaza border. Sinai insurgency largely suppressed by this point.

sources.citation: US DSCA Egypt 2023

2024.assessment:
US FMF at $1.3 billion continued. Egypt central to Gaza hostage negotiations
and ceasefire mediation. Egypt secured $8 billion IMF deal and UAE $35 billion
Ras el-Hekma investment, while also maintaining military cooperation with US.
Rafale additional order deliveries ongoing. Egypt-US military relationship
described as strategic by both sides.

sources.citation: US DSCA Egypt 2024

2025.assessment:
US FMF relationship projected to continue at approximately $1.3 billion annually.
Egypt remains a major non-NATO ally and core US security partner in the Middle
East and North Africa. French Rafale program continues. No major shifts in
military partnership framework anticipated.

sources.citation: US DSCA

1996.assessment:
Egypt hosted approximately 80,000 registered refugees and asylum seekers in 1996,
primarily Sudanese (largest group, fleeing Sudanese civil war), Somalis, and
Eritreans. Egypt's refugee population was significant given its geographic position
as a transit and destination country. The 1954 Refugee Convention (signed by Egypt)
gave UNHCR a role in refugee processing. Urban refugees in Cairo (Ain Shams,
Arba Wa Nus neighborhoods). No designated camps. Egypt did not produce significant
outflows as an origin country.

sources.citation: UNHCR Egypt Country Operations Plan 1996-1997
Human Rights Watch, Egypt: Prohibited Identities 1995

1997.assessment:
Refugee population growing, primarily Sudanese fleeing ongoing civil war. UNHCR
Cairo office was among the largest UNHCR urban operations globally. Egypt did not
operate formal refugee camps (policy of urban integration or neglect). Refugee
Convention applied by UNHCR as proxy given Egyptian government limitation on
formal asylum procedures.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 1997

1998.assessment:
Continued increase in refugee population, predominantly from Sudan and Somalia.
Egypt's geographic position as a transit corridor between sub-Saharan Africa and
Europe or the Gulf made it a significant destination. Sinai began emerging as a
smuggling route for people moving toward Israel. Urban refugee conditions in Cairo
were difficult with limited access to legal work.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 1998

1999.assessment:
Refugee population continued growing. The 1999 Comprehensive Peace Framework for
Sudan was not yet in effect; Sudanese conflict continued driving displacement.
UNHCR Egypt registered refugees from over 30 nationalities. Egypt remained
a primarily hosting country with no significant population producing refugee flows.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 1999

2000.assessment:
Registered refugee population exceeded 100,000 for the first time. Predominantly
Sudanese (perhaps 70% of registered refugees), with significant Somali, Eritrean,
Ethiopian, and Iraqi communities. UNHCR Cairo was handling over 6,000 new
registration applications per month. The urban refugee system was under severe
strain.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2000; UNHCR Egypt Annual Report

2001.assessment:
Population continued growing. Egyptian Mustafa Mahmoud Square protest by Sudanese
refugees in 2005 (ahead of timeline) foreshadowed growing community tensions.
Iraq War (2003 onwards) would later add Iraqi refugees. Egypt hosted the world's
largest urban refugee operation under UNHCR auspices.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2001

2002.assessment:
Continued growth of refugee population. Sudan peace negotiations beginning under
IGAD framework; eventual Comprehensive Peace Agreement would come in 2005.
Iraqi presence increasing as Saddam regime fell (2003 invasion approaching).
Egypt was among world's top 10 refugee-hosting countries in this period.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2002

2003.assessment:
Refugee population increased with Iraq War creating new displacement. Iraqi
refugees and asylum seekers began arriving in Egypt after the US invasion (March
2003). Sudanese population remained largest group. UNHCR Cairo became one of the
most complex urban refugee operations globally, handling multiple nationalities.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2003

2004.assessment:
Continued growth. Darfur crisis beginning to generate Sudanese displacement
(Darfur conflict began 2003). Iraqi community in Egypt growing. UNHCR handling
complex multi-nationality caseload. Egypt maintained policy of tolerating urban
refugees while not providing formal integration pathways.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2004

2005.assessment:
Registered refugee population declined somewhat as Sudan Comprehensive Peace
Agreement (January 2005) enabled some Sudanese repatriation. However, Darfur
displacement created new arrivals. The Mustafa Mahmoud Square tragedy (December
2005): Egyptian riot police forcibly evicted 2,500+ Sudanese refugees who had
camped for weeks; 27-30 refugees killed. This created an international crisis
and strained Egypt-UNHCR relations.

sources.citation: Mustafa Mahmoud Square crackdown, December 30 2005
UNHCR Global Trends 2005

2006.assessment:
Registered refugee population declined slightly as some Sudanese returned following
CPA. However, Iraq War violence intensified (sectarian conflict peak 2006-2007),
driving more Iraqis to Egypt. Iraqi community in Cairo became substantial —
estimated 100,000-150,000 (mostly middle-class professionals not necessarily
registered with UNHCR). Egyptian government reached bilateral agreement with UNHCR
for continued UNHCR-led RSD.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2006; UNHCR Egypt Country Operations

2007.assessment:
Registered population increased as Iraqi conflict displaced more people. Egypt
hosted approximately 100,000-150,000 Iraqis (registered and unregistered), plus
continuing Sudanese, Somali, and Eritrean communities. Egypt was a major regional
hub for refugee transit to third countries. UNHCR resettlement from Egypt to
US, Canada, Australia was a major pathway.

sources.citation: UNHCR Egypt Annual Report 2007; Global Trends 2007

2008.assessment:
Refugee population stabilized. Iraq Surge (2007) had begun reducing sectarian
violence and some Iraqis began returning home. Sinai crossing to Israel continued
(sub-Saharan Africans transiting Egypt to reach Israel). Egyptian border police
shootings of Sinai crossers documented by human rights groups. Egypt under
increasing Israeli and US pressure to stop illegal crossings.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2008; Human Rights Watch Sinai reports

2009.assessment:
Registered refugee population maintained at ~125,000. Darfur-related Sudanese
arrivals continuing. Somalia conflict generating new waves of Somali refugees.
